anatomy-of-an-llm-app: Here's a distinction that saves months of confusion: a model is not an app. A model is really just a function — you hand it some input, it hands back some output, and then it forgets you ever existed. The language model takes text and returns text. The voice model takes text and returns audio. The talking-head model takes audio and a picture and returns motion. That's all a model is: a stateless function. An app, on the other hand, is everything around that function — it remembers the conversation, decides which model to call and in what order, shows a user interface, stores the uploads, handles the errors when a model chokes. New builders think the model is the hard part. It isn't. The model is one ingredient; the app is the kitchen, the recipe, and the waiter. Most of your actual work in this course lives in the wiring, not in the models themselves.

The word that ties an LLM app together is orchestration. A single user request — make this picture say this line in this voice — is never one model call. It's a chain: understand the description, generate the speech, then drive the lips from that speech, then stitch it into video. Something has to stand over all of that like a conductor over an orchestra, deciding what plays when, taking the output of one model and feeding it as the input of the next, and catching it gracefully when a step fails. That something is the orchestrator, and it's ordinary code — loops, queues, error handling — not magic. In DenDen Studio there's a lovely twist: the language model itself writes the plan, in structured form, and then plain orchestration code executes that plan step by step. The brain decides; the hands carry it out. Keep those two roles separate in your head and the whole architecture stays clean.

We said it in lesson one, but here's the architectural reason open source is the backbone, not just a preference. When the model is open — the weights downloadable and the license permissive — you can run it, look inside it, and reshape it. Three things follow that a hosted API can never give you. One: control. No rate limit throttles your video, no surprise email announces your model is deprecated next month, and no bytes of a user's face leave your machine. Two: cost that scales with electricity, not with an invoice. Three, the quiet but crucial one: reproducibility. Pin an open model to a specific version and it behaves the same today, next month, and next year — which means your tests mean something and your bugs stay findable. A hosted model can shift under your feet silently. For an app that has to be trustworthy and debuggable, an open backbone isn't ideology — it's engineering.

Let's name the pieces you'll assemble, because this diagram is the course's table of contents. It starts with a description flowing into the language model, which produces a plan. From that plan, three families of media models do the heavy lifting: text-to-speech and voice cloning make the voice; a talking-head model makes the picture's lips move; and motion models handle the edits and extra animation. Finally, ffmpeg — the unglamorous, indispensable workhorse — stitches audio and frames into an actual video file. Every box on this diagram is a lesson or two, and every arrow is a piece of orchestration code you'll write. When a later lesson feels like it's floating in isolation, come back to this picture and find the box it's filling in. Nothing in this course is decoration — each piece has a home in this exact pipeline.

So here's the one sentence to carry out of this lesson: stateless models, stateful app, orchestration in between. The models forget everything the instant they answer; your app is what remembers and coordinates. Once that clicks, every new feature becomes a simple question — which models does it need, in what order, carrying what state? Answer that and you've designed the feature before writing a line of it. This is why we design the wiring first and slot the models in second: the wiring is the part that's actually yours, the part that makes DenDen Studio different from any other app calling the same open models. Get comfortable thinking this way now, because from the next lesson on we stop talking about the shape of apps and start building the pieces — beginning with the brain itself: running a real language model on your own machine.
animating-from-a-description: Read the creator's sentence again — she waves at the start, then leans in excitedly, hair blowing gently — and notice it is really two requests in one costume. The waving and the leaning are subject actions: precise, timed, performed by the avatar itself, and the creator will want to adjust them. The hair blowing is ambient motion: imprecise, continuous, atmospheric, and nobody will ever ask for the exact same gust twice. Two different problems demand two different machines. The first machine is a gesture library — named, parameterized motion templates riding the same coefficient channels the talking-head lesson gave us; the second is generative video diffusion, the AnimateDiff and Stable-Video family, which dreams a few seconds of motion from an image and a prompt. This is Act II of Usopp's Portrait Theater. The mayor wants the founding captain to gesture — greet the crowd, point to the harbor, react to the fireworks — and Usopp answers with a Move Book, while the atmosphere goes to a guest artist, the Painter of Winds. The whole lesson is learning which wish goes to which artist, and how to composite their work without gambling the one thing that must never move: the founder's face.

Usopp's Move Book grows straight out of his marionette scroll. Each page is one named move — Harbor Wave, Solemn Nod, The Captain's Point — written as rod-positions over time, with margins listing what can be tuned: bigger or smaller, faster or slower, left hand or right. In our system a gesture is exactly that: a reusable template over the head-pose and expression channels. Wave is a two-second curve set; nod-emphatic is a dip and return on the pitch axis; lean-in eases head-scale and pitch forward. Each exposes parameters — amplitude, duration, handedness, easing — and instantiating one at time t is pure arithmetic: scale the curves, shift them to t, merge them into the timeline. The library's virtues are a product's virtues. Predictable: the same wave every render, so creators build intuition and support can reproduce bugs. Editable: an instantiated gesture is data on the timeline, so Part Four's click-drag mutates it and make the wave bigger is one amplitude write. Composable: gestures layer. Cheap: only re-rendering costs GPU. The honest limit is that the library IS the vocabulary — and the discipline is curation, twenty to forty well-named moves covering what talking avatars actually do, not three hundred the LLM cannot choose between. Any stagehand can perform any page identically, any night; when the mayor wants the greeting grander, Usopp writes amplitude doubled in the margin, and it is exactly the same wave, grander.

The guest artist is a different machine entirely. The Painter of Winds animates atmosphere like no rod ever could — harbor banners rippling, lantern-light flickering on the painted sea, the captain's coat stirring in a breeze. In engineering terms, a video diffusion model extends image diffusion with temporal layers: AnimateDiff's insight was a plug-in motion module trained on video that snaps into existing image backbones, while the Stable-Video family is trained image-to-video natively. The image conditions the output, the text steers, and the model dreams a few seconds of coherent video. What text actually steers, in practice, is global and ambient motion — beautifully — because the model has seen oceans of hair blowing and candlelight flickering. What it steers poorly is precise, body-part-specific, timed action: ask for waves with her left hand at exactly two seconds and you get a wave, maybe, some hand, sometime, or a physically creative reinterpretation. And the costs are structural, not version-number problems. Clips run short. Identity is not guaranteed — the face can wander off-model mid-clip, alarming for a product whose entire premise is THIS face. Every generation is a fresh sample, so again but smaller is a new lottery ticket. And the output is pixels: no handles, no Part Four editing, ever. Given a one-line wish — evening wind, gentle, from the west — the Painter produces something beautiful, and never the same thing twice; ask for the same but softer and she shrugs: the wind does not repeat, and neither do I. Diffusion motion is a paintbrush, not a puppeteer.

The final staging is a composite Usopp diagrams for the crew. Who decomposes the creator's sentence? The LLM director, using exactly the machinery from Part One. The schema's gesture names are an enum of the actual library, so constrained decoding makes off-library gestures unpickable — the tool-registry allowlist, again. Timestamps are validated against clip duration with the same validate-plan bones. Ambient requests become free-text prompts for the generative layer, where imprecision is the accepted contract. And a dedicated unmatched field is the honesty channel: the model is explicitly told that silent approximation is worse than admitting no jig page. Then compositing stacks the layers. The talking-head render — speech plus aliveness — is the base. Gesture coefficients merge into its timeline before rendering, because they share the channel system; that is the whole reason the library lives on coefficients. Generative ambient motion renders as a separate pass and blends into background regions and hair edges, with masks derived from the intake detector's geometry. The masking frame is the load-bearing decision: it keeps the Painter's brush off the captain's face, because the one thing Act Two must never gamble is whether the founder still looks like the founder. The subject moves from the book, editable to the inch; the Painter owns the backdrop, gorgeous and unaccountable; and identity is never a diffusion sample's to repaint.

A real creator will eventually type she juggles three flaming torches, and there is no juggling page. Usopp's three answers become house policy. One: offer the nearest page, disclosed — no jig, but Festive Sway is close, want it livelier? Silent substitution feels like a bug; disclosed substitution feels like a collaborator. Two: commission the guest artist for a one-night special, marked as a take — generated variants, pick one, but these cannot be click-edited later. Three: if enough people keep asking, choreograph a proper jig page into the book next month, where demand proves out. What he refuses to do is improvise rod positions live for a move he never designed — the one time a stagehand tried, the captain's arm passed through his own hat and a child screamed. That is the forbidden path in code: never let the LLM freestyle coefficient values for unknown actions, because it will happily emit plausible-looking numbers that read as seizure rather than performance, and fail unreproducibly. On the compositing side, the merge policy that ships is additive with per-channel soft clamps, and every template begins and ends at zero delta so any merge is boundary-continuous — a nod during a lean sums, a triple stack saturates instead of snapping the neck. Carry two ideas forward. The gesture enum is not a limitation to apologize for; it is the same discipline as the tool registry — choose from what exists, parameterize within validated ranges, route everything else to honest fallbacks. And the asymmetry that justifies the whole architecture: instantiation is fifteen lines of arithmetic, while the equivalent generative operation is a GPU-minute and a lottery ticket. Constrained vocabulary plus honest fallback is how the feature stays magical instead of intermittently horrifying — which is the actual bar for shipping animation to non-experts.
backend-api-design: The Baratie's kitchen ran on chaos until Sanji bolted a spike by the pass and a board to the wall, and the very first thing that spike enforces is that a ticket only enters the system if it is a real order, checked at the pass — not whatever a flustered waiter scrawled on the way past. Our upload boundary is that pass. A creator drags in a file named vacation_photo.jpg, and the backend must not trust the extension, must not trust the declared content-type header, must not even trust the file size the browser reported, before treating those bytes as safe input to a vision model. The contract is concrete: read the first bytes and check the actual magic number against an allowlist — JPEG, PNG, WAV, MP3, the exact formats the multimodal and TTS lessons support — never whatever ffmpeg happens to parse, which is an attack surface wearing a feature's clothes. As the bytes stream to disk, compute the content hash incrementally, the very same hash the caching lesson keys stages on, so paying for it once at the door hands every downstream stage a verified key for free. Enforce the size ceiling while the bytes are still arriving, not after they finish, so a four-hundred-megabyte attack dies at the ceiling instead of filling the disk. And because a two-minute reference video for voice cloning is easily hundreds of megabytes, add resumability: fixed-size chunks tagged by offset, the server acking each one, the client asking what do you have on reconnect and resuming from the acked offset instead of byte zero. It is the pipeline's content-addressed caching, lifted one layer up to the transfer itself — nothing bespoke, just the resumable-transfer pattern applied to creator media.

A waiter at the Baratie writes the order, spikes it, and tells the customer a number and a wait — instantly, before a single fish is touched — because the ticket existing IS the promise that it will be cooked. That is 202 Accepted, exactly. A DenDen Studio render can take anywhere from seconds on a full cache hit to minutes when everything is cold, and nothing about that range fits a synchronous request-response: a POST /jobs that blocks until completion either times out the HTTP connection on the slow renders or wastes a held-open socket on the fast ones, and it gives the browser nothing to show while it waits. So POST /jobs does the oldest async-API move there is — it validates the plan through the director's unified validator, enqueues it on the job queue this course already built, and returns 202 Accepted with a job_id and a status_url, typically in under fifty milliseconds regardless of how long the render will actually run. The API layer's only job at that moment is bookkeeping: a row exists, its state is queued, and the creator is holding a number, like a customer watching the board. This turns every long operation into the same shape — create, then subscribe or poll, then fetch the result when done — which is why the director's preview-then-approve flow composes so cleanly with HTTP: approval calls POST /jobs once, and everything after that is status, never a held connection. And the corollary is the one architectural rule that keeps the whole product responsive: GET /jobs/{id} must always answer instantly no matter the job's state, because it reads one row and never touches the render itself — the status board stays readable even when the kitchen is slammed.

Zeff's line cooks do not get asked are you done — they move the ticket themselves, FIRE, then UP, then AWAY, and anyone in the room reads the state off the board without saying a word to a cook mid-plate. That board versus a waiter pestering the line is exactly Server-Sent Events versus polling. There are three ways a browser can learn a job moved from forty percent to sixty-five. Polling — GET /jobs/{id} every N seconds — is the simplest and needs no special infrastructure, but every poll is a full round trip, progress is only ever as fresh as the interval, and N has no good value: too short wastes requests on jobs sitting in queue, too long makes a fast render feel laggy. WebSockets give a true bidirectional channel, but a progress stream is not bidirectional — the client never needs to send anything after it subscribes — so a WebSocket buys duplex capability the app pays for in reconnect logic, ping-pong keepalives, and a stateful registry of open sockets, and then never uses. Server-Sent Events fit the actual traffic: one-directional, server to client, over plain HTTP. The browser's built-in EventSource handles reconnection automatically, the server just writes data-colon-JSON lines onto a kept-open response, and it degrades gracefully through the exact same load balancers and proxies that already handle regular HTTP, with no protocol-upgrade dance. Each event carries the job's stage name, percent, and an ETA from the cache-aware estimate, plus a monotonic event id — and that id is not decoration. It is what makes reconnection loss-free, which is the next slide's whole subject, and the reason SSE beats polling in the first place: polling has no recovery story, it just asks again and hopes nothing slipped past in between.

One restless night a rattled new waiter, sure the first order got lost, writes a SECOND ticket for the same table and spikes it too. Sanji catches it before it fires: he checks the table number and the exact order against the ticket already moving through FIRE, sees they match, and pulls the duplicate off the spike without a word to the kitchen — the table's food gets cooked exactly once. That is an idempotency key, in aprons. A creator's browser loses network for three seconds mid-submit, and the client, following ordinary retry logic, resends POST /jobs with the identical plan; without protection, this enqueues a second render, duplicate GPU minutes for work already started. The fix is an Idempotency-Key header the client generates once per logical submission — a UUID minted at click time, resent unchanged on every retry of THAT click. The server keeps a short-lived table of key to job_id: a repeated key with a matching plan hash returns the existing job_id with no new work, and a repeated key with a DIFFERENT plan hash is a client bug, rejected as a conflict, never silently guessed at. It is the API-layer sibling of the pipeline's content-addressed caching — there, identical stage inputs are free; here, identical submissions are free, one HTTP hop earlier. And every error the API returns uses one envelope, with a code, a message, and a retryable boolean — and that boolean is the whole point. A 5xx from a worker crash is retryable, nothing about the request was wrong; a 4xx from a validator rejection is not, retrying an invalid plan just fails again forever. Baking that into the payload, rather than leaving each client to infer retry policy from HTTP status folklore, is what keeps retry logic simple and correct on every client this API ever gets — the web app today, a CLI or mobile client tomorrow, all reading the same explicit signal.

Later a dockside storm rips two tickets clean off the spike mid-rush. Instead of guessing what was lost, Zeff has the waiters read the board's written sequence — forty-one, forty-two, forty-four spiked, forty-three missing — and only forty-three gets rewritten and re-spiked, at its correct spot in line, nothing re-fired that had already gone out, nothing skipped. That is Last-Event-ID recovery with a different kind of ink. On an SSE reconnect the client sends back the last event id it processed, the server replays every buffered event past that id from a short per-job ring buffer, then continues live — nothing missed, nothing re-delivered — and it works precisely because the ids are monotonic, which turns what did I miss into a well-defined query instead of replay-everything or start-from-now. Step back and none of this lesson was new theory: it is HTTP's oldest async patterns — 202 Accepted, idempotency keys, server-sent events — wired to the job queue and cache keys the course already built, applied to the specific shape of a media pipeline, long jobs, big files, and a creator who wants to watch it happen. The through-line is a single idea: split by time-cost. Creation, status, and result are separate concerns that each answer in constant time, because no single request-response cycle can be sized to fit both a millisecond cache hit and a two-minute cold render. And because everything in this course runs locally — one GPU worker pool, usually one machine — the queue depth IS the whole system's health signal, and the API should expose it honestly, three jobs ahead of you, rather than pretend infinite capacity. But local changes only capacity planning and a few defaults; it never discounts the contracts. A locally-run vision model fed a malformed file is just as exploitable as a cloud one, so the upload-validation and idempotency discipline stay non-negotiable. Local changes the deployment topology, never the correctness obligations.
capstone-denden-studio: A creator uploads a photo and a voice sample, writes make her wave and welcome viewers, warm and excited, and clicks go. Follow that one request through everything this course built. Upload: the file streams in chunked and resumable, its bytes verified against a magic-number allowlist, its content hash computed at the door — from backend-api-design — and that hash becomes the cache key every downstream stage will check against. Intake: a VLM reads the photo under the semantics-versus-geometry jurisdiction rule, a deterministic face detector measures the geometry neither should guess at, and the combined verdict decides the pipeline path — from multimodal-models. Voice: the sample is validated for quality and duration, and before anything else touches it, the consent registry checks this voice_id is enrolled, unrevoked, and in scope for this use — from voice-cloning-from-a-sample and safety-consent-ethics. Direction: the creator's paragraph, plus a grounding block of real project facts pulled through read-only tools, becomes one schema'd plan document — stages, voice, gesture timeline, ambient prompt, parameters — from llm-as-director. The plan hits the unified validator; policy violations like an out-of-scope voice reject outright, encoding mistakes like an out-of-bounds keyframe repair in a bounded loop. The creator sees a plain-language preview with a cache-aware cost estimate and approves. Execution: the approved plan, verbatim, never re-generated, goes to the job queue; each stage computes a content key from its actual inputs, checks the cache, and only runs what changed — from media-pipeline-orchestration and hardware-quantization-caching. TTS, coefficients or gesture instantiation, frame rendering, and mux run in dependency order, each hashed and cached the moment it completes. Delivery: progress streams to the browser over SSE with real stage names and percentages, the frontend renders one plan object across every panel — from creator-frontend — and the finished video is tagged with a signed provenance manifest and an in-media watermark before it ever reaches export, from safety-consent-ethics. Evaluation runs in the background the whole time: sync offset, audio and frame quality proxies on this exact render, feeding the golden-set trend line the team watches across every model version, from evaluating-generated-media. One request, every lesson, one flow.

Now step back from the single request and look at the whole map: seven components, each with a narrow contract. The frontend holds one plan object and renders every view from slices of it; it talks to nothing but the API. The backend API accepts uploads and job submissions, returns 202 instantly, streams progress, and enforces idempotency — it holds no domain logic of its own, only contracts. The director, an LLM, grounded and constrained, is the only component that turns free text into a plan; it never touches a file, a model weight, or a queue directly. The executor, deterministic workers on the job queue, is the only component that runs models and writes artifacts; it takes plans, never prose. The model pool — Whisper, a TTS engine, a talking-head model, a VLM, sized against the hardware lesson's residency plan — does the actual generation, swappable behind each stage's contract without the executor caring which checkpoint is loaded. The consent and provenance layer gates every synthesis call and tags every output, sitting between the executor and the model pool where nothing can route around it. And the evaluation harness watches everything downstream of the executor, on every render automatically and on a human-reviewed sample, feeding findings back into both the proxy-metric set and the golden set. Draw the arrows and a rule falls out: prose flows in one direction, creator to director to plan, and executable instructions flow in exactly one direction from there, plan to executor to model pool. Nothing downstream of the plan ever produces a new plan or a new prose interpretation — a failure replans by going back to the director with error context, never by improvising at the executor. That single-direction flow is what makes every other invariant in this course enforceable, because you cannot police a boundary that data crosses back and forth across freely. That is the whole architecture: not a pile of models, but a set of narrow contracts with a one-way current running through them.

Five patterns appear, restated, in nearly every lesson from Part One onward, and that repetition was deliberate, not filler. Jurisdiction — semantic judgment to probabilistic components, geometric or deterministic facts to deterministic ones — appears in multimodal intake, in Whisper-plus-voice-activity-detection, in the director-versus-executor split itself, because the same mistake, letting a model guess at something a formula should compute or the reverse, is available at every layer, so the guardrail has to be re-asserted at every layer. Content-addressed caching — hash the real inputs, key the output, skip identical work — appears in stage execution, in the director's cost estimates, in the API's idempotency table, in the golden-set regression diff: one mechanism, reused because have I already done this exact work is a question every layer independently needs answered. Propose, never execute — the LLM emits documents, deterministic code runs them — is the director's entire boundary and also the frontend's relationship to the plan and the API's relationship to a client request; probabilistic judgment stays upstream of anything with real cost or consequence, everywhere it appears. Reject versus repair — fix encoding mistakes, refuse policy violations, and check policy first — shows up in the director's validator and in the consent gate identically, because both need the same asymmetry for the same reason. And scoped, revocable authorization — a grant checked fresh, never assumed — shows up in consent and in idempotency keys and in review triggers; every gate in this system is a small, fast, always-fresh lookup, never a cached assumption. Seeing these five patterns once, in one lesson, would have looked like a clever trick. Seeing them five times, in five different lessons, each time solving a different concrete problem, is what shows they are actual architecture — the load-bearing walls, not decoration repeated for effect. If a new engineer asks whether this is duplicated logic to refactor away, the answer is that the hash function belongs in one utility, but the decision to check freshness before trusting a fact is not duplication — it is the same architectural choice independently defending each layer against its own version of the same risk.

Now, for anyone actually building this: a capstone build does not need every model integration to prove the architecture — it needs the full loop, narrow. The minimum viable slice is one path through intake, a single reference-photo category, skip the rest; one TTS engine, skip voice cloning's extra validation; one talking-head model, skip the gesture-library and diffusion families; the complete director to validate to preview to execute to cache chain with real content-hashing, not stubbed, because the caching behavior is exactly what needs proving; one consent check, even a hardcoded single-voice registry; SSE progress end to end; and one proxy metric, sync offset, running automatically. That slice touches every architectural boundary in this course while implementing only a fraction of the model surface, and it is the version worth building first, because it proves or disproves the plan-document-as-lingua-franca bet before a single hour goes into a second talking-head model or a third voice engine. Build order follows what unblocks the most, not what is most interesting or easiest. Content-addressed caching and the job queue come first, because nearly every other component — the director's cost estimates, the API's status reads, the evaluation harness's regression diffs — assumes hashed, cached stages exist beneath it, so building it last means retrofitting that assumption into everything built before it. The plan schema comes next, before any UI, because the frontend, the director, and the executor all need to agree on its shape before any of the three can be usefully built in parallel. The director and a bare-bones executor come before the polished frontend, because a working backend reachable by curl proves the loop; a beautiful UI over a broken loop proves nothing. Consent and provenance are not an add-on-later phase — that is the safety lesson's pitfall restated at project scale — the minimal versions belong in the first working slice, because retrofitting a gate into a system already shipping ungated media is a strictly harder problem than building the gate in from the start. And if time runs short, cut model variety, one voice, one talking-head family, long before cutting any of the five invariants: a narrow system with every guardrail intact is a real foundation; a broad system missing a guardrail is a liability wearing a feature list.

The day the Thousand Sunny finally leaves Water Seven is the day every system this crew spent an arc building has to work together for the first time, not separately — the hull rides true because Galley-La's stitched marks are the real ones, checked, not re-litigated mid-voyage; the stores hold because the provisioning tickets were trusted, not re-counted crate by crate; and when a squall hits, nobody improvises a new plan from scratch, they run the course correction through the same roles and checks that built the ship. That is the capstone at full scale. Two last practical things before the loop begins. First, size the box before writing the executor: a live-mode session needs the co-resident VRAM sum of whatever runs simultaneously, held for the session; a batch render needs only the peak of any single stage, since stages run sequentially and release VRAM between them — the hardware lesson's max-not-sum rule. Storage follows the caching manifest directly: renders per week times artifact size times retention window, with eviction-by-cost-to-remake as the release valve when the estimate is wrong, which it usually is, in the direction of creators rendering more than the spreadsheet assumed. The one number worth computing before anything else is cold-cache time-to-first-render, end to end, because if that is not acceptable on the target hardware, no amount of caching or UI polish fixes it later — caching only ever helps the second render of anything, never the first. Second, every lesson's evaluation habit becomes a standing job once this ships: the golden set refreshed against real anonymized usage on a schedule, not once at launch; model upgrades run through the full regression harness with the old model shadowed during a canary period; consent grants expiring and re-confirmed on the registry's own schedule; the audit log's retention enforced on a timer, not by cleanup someone forgets. None of that is new work invented for maintenance — it is the same golden-set, cache-key, and scoped-grant machinery built for day-one correctness, now running continuously instead of once. Watching the coastline disappear, Usopp says what the whole crew is thinking: we built each piece separately and argued about every detail, and somehow, today, it is just one ship. That is the honest way to think about finishing a capstone build: it does not finish. It starts the loop that keeps every invariant this course built from quietly rotting the moment nobody is watching it anymore. Twenty-two lessons, one system, and now it is yours to build.
click-drag-correction: The portrait theater's third act opens with a communication breakdown. The mayor keeps requesting fixes in words — the pointing should be more toward the harbor, and prouder, but not so much — and after four rounds of Usopp adjusting rod-numbers and the mayor squinting, Franky rebuilds the interface overnight. His invention is guide-handles: every adjustable rod now ends in a brass grip the mayor can simply take, move the painted arm where he wants it, and the rig writes the correction automatically. That is direct manipulation, and it earns its name through four promises. Visible equals grabbable: whatever the creator can see wrong — a keyframe dot, a motion-path anchor, a clip's edge — must be the literal thing the pointer grabs, with no select-the-object-then-find-its-dialog-then-locate-the-field indirection. Immediate feedback: the dragged thing follows the pointer and the consequence previews live — the portrait's pose changes as the mayor moves it — because past about a hundred milliseconds of lag the object stops feeling held and starts feeling remote-controlled. Constraints act during the drag: the rods stiffen at the joints' safe limits, so pulling the arm past where a shoulder bends simply stops, firmly and wordlessly. As Franky says, the rig argues with your hand, not with your ears — joint limits and snaps and axis locks shape the motion as it happens, instead of letting you place an illegal value and scolding you after. And release commits exactly one thing: only when the mayor lets go does the rig ink one clean correction onto the overlay — his whole adjustment as a single mark, which Nami can peel off whole. One grab, one mark, one regret. In DenDen Studio these promises play out across three views — a curve view, a spatial view over the video, and a timeline of clips — but all three write the same records the LLM will write next lesson. That convergence was the design test, and this lesson is where it pays.

Underneath the feel is a stack of coordinate transforms, and this is where almost every drag bug is born. A pointer event arrives in screen pixels. Transform one: the canvas may be CSS-scaled and sits at an offset, so subtract the canvas origin and scale by devicePixelRatio — retina displays render the canvas at two or three times its CSS size, and if you forget this, every hit lands half an inch off; that is the second-most classic bug. Transform two: the video preview is drawn letterboxed inside the canvas, so map into video-frame coordinates, the space where landmarks and motion paths live — deliberately the same pixels the intake detector already uses. For the curve view, transform three maps pixels to domain values: x to time, y to channel value. And here lives the classic bug of them all. Screen Y grows downward while every value axis grows upward, so the mapping must invert — and Franky learned this the hard way when his apprentice mounted the overlay scroll behind the translucent master instead of in front, reading every correction mirrored: the mayor dragged the arm toward the harbor and the recorded fix moved it toward the tavern. One flipped bracket later, Franky adds a ritual to every setup — grab a test handle, drag it up, confirm the mark goes up — every view, day one, no exceptions. The discipline that keeps the whole stack sane: write each transform as a pure function with its inverse, screen-to-value paired with value-to-screen, compose them rather than inlining arithmetic in event handlers, and round-trip test that inverse of forward of a point returns the point, at devicePixelRatio one and two, zoomed and scrolled. Ten lines of test catch the entire bug class, because when a fix lands somewhere else, the answer is always one of these functions. Every haunted rig Franky ever fixed was a mirror somewhere in the linkage.

Franky's engineering hides in the feel, starting with hit-testing. A keyframe dot renders at four pixels, and nobody can reliably click a four-pixel target; on touch, a fingertip is forty CSS pixels of ambiguity. So hit-testing is nearest-within-radius: measure the distance from the pointer to every candidate — in the candidate's own coordinate space, post-transform, because testing screen-space pointer against untransformed positions is a bug all its own — accept the nearest within a tolerance of eight to twelve pixels for a pointer, twenty or more for touch. Franky's grips are fat for exactly this reason: harbor hands miss thin handles. When candidates overlap, break ties by priority. The already-selected object wins, so a fine adjustment near a neighbor does not steal the grab — when two grips sit close, the rig favors the one the mayor grabbed last, so tuning the wrist never accidentally grabs the elbow. Then smaller, harder targets beat larger ones: a keyframe dot beats the curve segment under it, the curve beats the background pan. And for paths and curves, distance is point-to-segment, not point-to-anchor, because creators grab the line between keys expecting to bend it. Affordances close the loop before any click. Hover swells the dot and changes the cursor, so grabbability is advertised, not discovered by trial. The grabbed object visibly attaches — color, a slight scale. A ghost of the original position stays faintly visible during the drag, so the mayor sees before and after in one glance — the A-B instinct, live, exactly the chalk ghost Franky's rig holds beside the moved arm. And constraint proximity telegraphs: the snap target glows as you approach, the clamp boundary draws as a subtle wall. None of this is decoration; every affordance answers a question the creator would otherwise answer by clicking wrong — what can I grab, what am I holding, where was it, and what happens if I release.

The drag loop is a small state machine, and every commitment in it matters. Press: hit-test, and on a hit record the anchor — pointer position, the object's original value, a timestamp — then capture the pointer, because drags continue outside the canvas and losing the object at the window edge is amateur hour. Move: transform pointer to domain space, then apply constraints in domain space — clamp to the channel's limits, the same low and high the render composite enforces, so the drag can never even propose what the renderer would reject; axis-lock if shift is held; and snap to frame boundaries or neighbor keys within a snap radius, with snapping applied after clamping, because snapping to a target outside the legal range would un-clamp. Then update the provisional value and request a preview recomposite — throttled to animation frames, recomputing only the affected channel over only the affected time range, the data model's sparsity making the under-a-hundred-milliseconds promise affordable. Release: discard the provisional state and write one edit record — channel, delta keys of new minus original, a default easing, provenance drag — through the same API the LLM editor will use next lesson. Escape cancels for free: the provisional state vanishes and nothing was ever written. Two commitments deserve emphasis. Provisional-until-release keeps the edit list clean — no forty-records-per-drag pollution, no undo-undoes-one-pixel, and cancel is free because nothing was committed. And constraints-in-domain-space means the physics is real: the drag literally cannot exceed a joint limit, because the transform clamps before the preview ever sees the value — the handle visibly stiffens at the wall, teaching the limit by feel. Franky's rehearsal-proven poses are cut as shallow detents into the guide tracks: near a detent the handle tugs gently and clicks home, and holding against the pull glides past it for the times the mayor truly wants between. Compare the alternative everyone builds once — free drag, validate on release, error toast, value out of range. Same rule delivered as rejection instead of physics, and that single choice, repeated everywhere, is the difference between a tool that feels like clay and a tool that feels like a form with a strict clerk.

Step back and see what carries forward. Direct manipulation is a discipline, not a widget: the visible thing is the grabbable thing, feedback lands within a perceptual deadline of about a hundred milliseconds, constraints act during the drag as physics rather than after it as error messages, and release commits exactly one undoable record. Miss any one promise and the tool stops feeling like clay. The bugs concentrate in two places, and knowing that makes you fast. The transform stack is the first — inverted Y and unscaled devicePixelRatio are the classics — caught by round-trip tests and the drag-up-confirm-up ritual taped to the monitor. Hit-testing is the second — testing in the wrong space, or missing priority so the easy big target steals from the small hard one that was almost always the intent — caught by a debug overlay that renders every hit region and the transformed pointer live, because hit bugs are invisible in code review and obvious on screen. When the preview cannot keep up, degrade fidelity before responsiveness: freeze the video frame and animate only the overlay — the curves, the path, the landmarks — because a laggy direct manipulation is worse than an honest indirect one; the hand corrects against stale feedback and oscillates. That is the live pipeline's degradation ladder, transplanted into the editor: fixed responsiveness, negotiable fidelity. And the payoff that ties Part Four together: the mouse and the LLM are two input devices emitting identical records into the same edit list. Undo, A-B, provenance, and survival-across-regeneration all come from the data model, not from either device. Howard finally conceded that every haunted robot has a mirror in it somewhere; Franky told his apprentice the same. Get the transforms honest and the records uniform, and the next lesson — teaching an LLM to write into this exact list — is one more hand on the same clay.
creator-frontend: Usopp's workshop has three stations that all need to agree on one design at once: the sketch pinned to the board, the half-built prop on the workbench, and Franky's materials ledger tracking what the build will cost. Early on Usopp made the rookie mistake of letting each station keep its own notion of the design — he would adjust a measurement on the sketch, forget to update the workbench mock-up, and by the time Franky priced materials against the stale mock-up the whole afternoon's work disagreed with itself. That drift is the single biggest source of frontend bugs in an editor this shaped: the timeline says a gesture is at three-point-two seconds, the preview renders it at three-point-oh, because each view kept its own copy and only one got updated. Robin's fix is our fix: there is exactly one master sketch, pinned centrally, and every station is a window onto it, never a copy. The frontend holds exactly one plan object in memory — the same document the director emits and the executor consumes — and every visible thing, the timeline's clip blocks, the preview canvas, the cost estimate, the gesture list, is a pure render of slices of that one object. A drag handler does not move the clip on screen; it mutates plan-dot-stages-i-dot-start, and every view subscribed to that path re-renders. It is the same discipline as the pipeline's content-addressed caching and the director's single-document design, one more layer up: one canonical state, many disposable views of it. And it makes undo trivial and safe, because every edit is a mutation of one plan object, undo is restore the previous snapshot, not reverse this specific UI action and pray every dependent view noticed — the edit-list architecture from the direct-manipulation lesson already stores edits as an ordered, toggleable log for exactly this reason.

When Usopp drags a measurement line on the sketch, the workbench mock-up updates its shape immediately, right under his hand, because he is trusted to eyeball a length correctly — that adjustment does not wait on anyone's approval. That is optimistic UI. A creator dragging a gesture's timing expects the block to move under their cursor at sixty frames a second, and waiting for a server round trip on every pixel of drag would feel broken regardless of how fast the backend is. So local, in-bounds edits — drag position, an amplitude slider, a delete — apply to the in-memory plan immediately and render instantly; the change is queued for a debounced sync, not on every pixel but on drag-release, matching the direct-manipulation lesson's one-commit-per-release rule, which runs it through the same unified validator the director's output goes through. If validation fails a constraint the UI did not enforce locally — some cross-field coherence rule — the screen reconciles: the plan snaps back to the last server-confirmed state and the creator sees why, not a silent revert. But when Usopp calls out an entirely new, untested mechanism — what if it launches two things at once — the crew does not build it on a hunch; Franky checks it against what the ship's materials can actually support before a single plank is cut, because that kind of change can fail in ways an eyeball cannot catch. So not every edit deserves optimism. A drag stays entirely local because its failure modes are boring and rare. Submitting a natural-language edit request, or approving a render plan, is not optimistic — those go through the full round trip because their outcomes are uncertain enough that showing a guess and correcting it later would be worse than a brief, honest wait. The rule: optimistic for edits whose validation the UI can mostly replicate; pessimistic for anything whose validity depends on server-side facts the UI cannot see. Get it backwards — optimistic on the natural-language edit, pessimistic on the drag — and you have built exactly the sluggish, second-guessing interface creators complain about.

During a long, complicated brew-and-cure step that Franky insists on watching, Usopp does not just sit there — the workshop board shows exactly what phase it is in: curing, eleven minutes left, this batch reuses last week's resin mix so it is faster than usual, instead of a vague working sign, so nobody panics or interrupts. The backend lesson built a progress stream carrying real stage names and percentages specifically so the frontend never has to show a generic spinner during a two-minute render. A spinner communicates something is happening and nothing else; a progress state built from the same events — stage frames, percent sixty-two, eta eight seconds — can say rendering frames, sixty-two percent, about eight seconds left, and when a stage is a cache hit it can say so, voice and coefficients reused, this will be fast, which is the trust-building savings line the director lesson designed the preview around, now paying off a second time in the progress UI. This only works because the frontend subscribes to the same event stream the backend was built to deliver — it is not a separate progress-estimation heuristic living in the UI, guessing independently, which would visibly lie the first time a creator hits a genuinely cold, slow render. The other discipline is distinguishing waiting for the network from waiting for the GPU from queued behind other jobs. A creator staring at queued, two jobs ahead of you, understands their situation and can walk away; a creator staring at an undifferentiated spinner for the same two minutes assumes something is broken and reloads the page — which, if the frontend is not built on the SSE reconnect discipline from the backend lesson, can actually lose their place in the stream. The honest progress state is not politeness; it is what keeps a creator from fighting the tool during the exact moments the tool is working hardest.

And crucially, while that cure is running, Usopp keeps sketching the next prop entirely unbothered — nothing about one station being busy locks the other two. That is scoped locking, and it is the difference between an editor that feels responsive and one that feels frozen. The laziest way to handle an in-flight request is a full-screen loading overlay that blocks every interaction until it resolves, and it is also the most damaging to a creator's sense of control: if adjusting a gesture's amplitude triggers a debounced sync, the correct scope of disabled is that one slider's immediate feedback — maybe a subtle pending indicator on it — not the timeline, not the preview, not the unrelated voice-selection panel three tabs over. Locking should be scoped to the smallest control whose precondition is genuinely unmet: the Approve and Render button disables while a plan is invalid or a preview is loading, which is its actual precondition, but the gesture timeline underneath stays fully editable, because editing gestures has no dependency on the render button's state. This scoping discipline is what makes a complex multi-panel editor feel responsive even when parts of it are legitimately busy — a creator adjusting voice settings while a previous render's progress stream is still running in a corner widget should never notice the two are related, because architecturally they are not. And the plan document's field-level granularity, the same structure the optimistic-UI section relies on, is exactly what makes this possible: lock state can live at the same granularity as the fields it protects, instead of the coarse the-app-is-busy boolean that a full-screen spinner implies. Sheldon's old habit of treating the whole lab as busy was never a fact about the work; it was a fact about his state management, and Amy's one-spec rule is what let calibration run in the corner while the grant proposal kept being drafted, untouched and unblocked.

Both workshops arrive at the same rules the hard way, and both scenes end on the same detail on purpose: work continuing unblocked elsewhere while one station is legitimately busy. Step back and this frontend exists to support three trust-building moments the whole backend was built for: the upload verdict — did my file pass, told honestly at the door; the pre-render preview — here is what it will cost and what it will reuse, before you commit; and the post-edit echo — here is exactly what changed, reflected instantly. The screen is where the entire pipeline from Parts one through six either becomes invisible or leaks through as confusion. When a server-side validator rejects an optimistically-applied edit, the correct behavior is not a silent revert — the creator just watched their edit undone by an invisible hand, which reads as the app being broken. The plan snaps back to the last confirmed value and surfaces why, using the same named-value error discipline as the director's repair loop: gesture time must stay within the clip's duration, zero to eight-point-two seconds, attached to the snapped-back control. The retryable and the specific reason travel all the way from the unified validator to the exact control the creator was touching, instead of dying at the network boundary as an unexplained bounce. And the payoff of building the plan document as the shared language across the director, the executor, and the frontend is this: when the creator is not a human clicking but another program calling the API, very little changes — a script submits the same plan JSON through the same POST /jobs, idempotency key and all, and reads the same SSE stream. Optimistic UI and locking simply do not apply — there is no screen to update, no control to disable — but the error envelope's retryable field matters more, not less, because a programmatic client's retry logic has no human eyeballing a try-again dialog. Build the UI as a thin, disposable view over the plan contract rather than baking business logic into event handlers, and a CLI or automation client is never a second implementation of anything — it is the same one-source-of-truth discipline paying off at the API boundary instead of the render-tree boundary.
evaluating-generated-media: Before every long voyage Franky runs the Thousand Sunny through sea trials, and he built real instruments for it: a pressure gauge on the hull, a listing meter that flags if she rides crooked in the water, a strain reading on the mast lines. Every instrument runs on every trial, automatically, and catches exactly what it was built to catch — a listing meter will flag a hull riding two degrees off true, instantly, on the very first pass, no argument. Those gauges are proxy metrics. A proxy metric is a number a machine can compute in milliseconds that correlates with quality without directly measuring it, and sync offset is the clearest example for this pipeline: cross-correlate the audio envelope against the generated mouth-shape signal, and the offset in milliseconds where they align best tells you whether the render drifted out of sync, running on every single render for near-zero marginal cost. Audio has its own proxies — signal-to-noise, clipping detection, a speech-quality model score — and so does frame output, a no-reference sharpness or artifact score catching a stage that silently degraded resolution. But the discipline is knowing exactly what each proxy cannot see. What the listing meter will not flag — what Franky learned the hard way after a perfect reading trial where the ship still felt subtly wrong on open water — is whether the Sunny sails well. Sync offset can detect that the mouth moves at the wrong time; it cannot detect that the mouth moves the wrong shape while staying perfectly on-beat. A render that is numerically in-sync and visually wrong, dead-eyed, robotic articulation, passes every proxy check clean. This is not a flaw to fix by finding a better proxy — it is a structural ceiling: proxies measure what they were built to measure, and does this look natural was never one of the things sync-offset measures. Treating a clean proxy score as the video is good, rather than the video is not obviously broken in this one specific way, is the single most common evaluation mistake this lesson exists to prevent.

The director lesson built a golden set of request paragraphs with assertions about the resulting plan; this lesson extends the same pattern one stage further, to the resulting media. A golden set here is a fixed collection of representative inputs — a range of reference images, frontal, profile, glasses, varied lighting, from the intake lesson's categories, scripts of varying length and emotional tone, several cloned and stock voices — run through the full pipeline on every candidate model or config change, with proxy metrics computed automatically on every output: sync offset, audio quality score, frame-artifact rate, render time, VRAM peak. A model upgrade, a new talking-head checkpoint or a different vocoder, is not judged by the demo clip looked good; it is judged by whether the golden set's proxy scores moved, and in which direction, across the whole representative range, not just the one input someone happened to eyeball. And this is exactly why the golden set has to be deliberately varied rather than one known-good reference case: a golden set built from one easy, well-lit, neutral-tone reference will show clean proxy scores on every model version forever, regardless of real quality changes elsewhere, because it never exercises the paths where models actually differ or degrade. A profile shot exercises different code paths than a frontal shot in most talking-head architectures; a high-emotion script exercises articulation range a flat neutral script never touches; a cloned voice exercises the zero-shot path differently than a stock voice. A regression that only shows up on profile shots is invisible to a golden set with no profile shots in it, and ships silently. This is what makes evaluation tractable at the pace models actually improve: a proxy-metric regression on the golden set is a five-minute automated check that catches the new lip-sync model is twice as fast but drifts on profile shots before it ships, the same way the hardware lesson's golden-set gating caught quantization quality loss before it reached creators. The harness does not replace human judgment — it is the filter that keeps human review focused on genuine candidates instead of burning review time on regressions an automated check would have caught in seconds.

Naturalness, emotional appropriateness, and does this look like a real person are judgments no current automated metric reliably makes — they need a human, but watching every render a system produces does not scale past a demo. So sea trials run in two tiers: every gauge on every trial, catching hull angle and strain instantly and for free, and Franky personally standing watch for a full lap around the bay on any trial where a gauge reads borderline, plus one random lap a month even when every gauge reads clean, specifically hunting for the wrongness no gauge has ever caught. That is stratified sampling. Review one hundred percent of a specific, narrow slice where mistakes are costliest — every render that failed a proxy's soft threshold, a borderline sync score, an unusual frame-artifact rate, gets a mandatory look, since close to the line is exactly where proxies are least trustworthy. Add a random few-percent slice of otherwise-clean renders, to catch the failure mode proxies cannot see at all, technically perfect sync and visually dead performance. Add a fixed slice whenever a model or pipeline version changes, because the golden set's proxy pass is necessary but not sufficient; a human still watches a sample before a version goes from staging to default. And reviewers need a rubric, not a vibe, or two reviewers score the same clip differently and the signal is noise. A short structured scorecard — mouth-shape naturalness one to five, emotional match to the script's tone one to five, any obvious artifact yes or no with a category, overall would this embarrass the creator yes or no — turns subjective review into a trend line comparable across reviewers and across time, the same way the director's golden-set assertions turned does the plan look right into something a diff could show. Penny, roped in as a deliberately non-expert second opinion specifically because Sheldon trusts his own eye too much to catch his own blind spots, turns out to have a genuinely good, consistent eye for that one looks off even when she cannot articulate the physics — and when she flags something the drift sensor missed, Sheldon's response is not to dismiss her, it is to go figure out what new number could have caught it. Because a repeatable human judgment the instruments miss is, to him, just a proxy metric that has not been invented yet — and that is human review's real job: not just gatekeeping, but discovering the next proxy metric worth building.

It is tempting to keep chasing better proxies until human review is unnecessary; it will not fully happen, and knowing why shapes the product. Does this look natural is not a measurement gap waiting on a smarter model — it is asking whether a synthetic performance matches an enormous, culturally-specific, context-dependent human intuition for how real people move and sound, the same reason the multimodal lesson drew a hard jurisdiction line between semantic judgment, which is VLM territory, and geometric measurement, which is deterministic detector territory. Sync offset, artifact detectors, and quality scores all live firmly on the geometric side of that line; does this feel right lives on the semantic side, and no amount of proxy-metric sophistication moves a geometric measurement into semantic territory. When Sheldon initially insists a low-enough drift number is the answer, Leonard shows him two nights with nearly identical drift scores producing visibly different image quality — the number was honest, it just never measured the thing that differed. Designing around that ceiling rather than against it means the product never promises fully-automated quality assurance. The automated layer's honest job is triage: catch the cheap, obvious, high-volume failures before any human looks. The human layer's honest job is judgment on the sample that survives triage. This is the same two-tier structure as the misuse-review lesson's hard-block-list-plus-human-queue, applied to quality instead of safety: automate what is automatable with high confidence, route genuine ambiguity to a human, and resist the temptation to expand the automated tier past where it can actually see. A proxy metric stretched to claim more than it measures produces confident, wrong answers, which is worse for trust than an honest this needs a human look. And this is why a single opaque quality-six-point-two-out-of-ten judge-model score is not the answer either: when it drops, there is no path from the number to the cause, because it conflates timing, artifacts, naturalness, and emotional match into one undifferentiated signal — worth having as one more proxy in the battery, never as the sole arbiter, because the entire value of the two-tier design is that every layer's honest scope is known.

Both stories draw the exact line this lesson draws, and both endings matter most. The instant automatic gauges — hull angle, mast strain, tracking drift — are proxy metrics: cheap, run on everything, catching precisely and only what they were built to catch. Franky's ear on deck and Penny's look at the images are the human layer, deployed on a sample, because watching or listening to everything does not scale and most of it is redundant with what the gauges already confirmed. And both crews are explicit that a clean instrument reading is not the final verdict — sails well and looks crisp are judgments the instruments structurally cannot make. The whole harness is those two tiers interacting in both directions: tier one filters what reaches tier two, so human time is spent on genuine candidates instead of everything, and tier two feeds back into tier one — a failure pattern human review catches repeatedly becomes a candidate for a new proxy metric, so the automated tier's coverage grows over time instead of staying fixed at launch. That feedback loop is the part both stories end on deliberately: catching something only a human caught is never a one-off, it becomes the search for a new instrument, the harness improving itself. But a harness is a system with its own failure modes, not a fixed instrument installed once, so guard it against three decay modes: golden-set staleness, refreshed from a real anonymized usage sample on a schedule as creator inputs drift; threshold drift, caught by tracking the review-queue's trigger rate as its own monitored metric and re-calibrating when it strays from target; and reviewer calibration drift, caught by periodic inter-rater checks and occasionally re-inserting a known-bad clip to confirm reviewers still catch it. And when the hardest case arrives — a model upgrade that improves every golden-set proxy score across the board while creator complaints increase afterward — the investigation starts from that exact gap: either a coverage gap, the upgrade helps on what the golden set measures but hurts on an input type outside its range, which is the more common root cause and gets checked first, or a proxy-blind-spot regression, better timing and artifacts but degraded naturalness, invisible to tier one by construction. Either way the fix is never trust the proxies more — it is expanding the golden set's coverage or the proxy battery's scope, using the real complaints as the specification for what was missing.
hardware-quantization-caching: Before the crew's longest crossing, the Sunny becomes a lesson in fitting. Nami runs the manifest with a shipwright's coldness: everything that boards gets weighed — not catalog weights, actual scale weights, because the catalog does not know how much water your rope drinks — and the hold's capacity is a number on the wall, with a red margin line above it that no one is allowed to plan into. Part Zero taught the arithmetic — co-residency costs the sum, sequencing costs the max plus load latency — and the shipping product needs that arithmetic turned into named policies. DenDen Studio has three modes with different working sets, just as Nami's stowage is not one plan but three. Edit mode: the director LLM and the VLM should answer in under a second, so keep the LLM warm always because it is the interaction loop, the VLM warm during active sessions, media models cold. Batch render: the worker runs stages sequentially, load, run, release, keeping at most the two models the plan uses back-to-back — a six-second model load amortizes fine over a ninety-second frames stage and terribly over a two-second mux. Live mode: the co-residency sum, small variants, everything warm, nothing else allowed on the card. The mechanism under all three is the same: a model manager owning load and unload with LRU-by-usage and per-mode pin lists — the worker asks for a model, the manager loads it, evicting the least-recently-used unpinned model if the ledger would overflow. Two disciplines keep it honest. The ledger's numbers are measured — load the model, read the allocator, write it down, because spec sheets lie by the working-memory margin. And mode transitions are explicit events: Nami's battle mode is a pre-drawn list executed before the enemy closes, because re-stowing during a broadside is how ships die. Entering live mode evicts to the live pin list before the session starts, not when the first over-budget allocation crashes mid-performance.

Sanji's contribution is compression, applied with a chef's discrimination. Rice, beans, stock bases — dried and pressed to a quarter volume, and in a stew you will never know. But the crossing's single celebration dinner travels full — the fresh cut, the real butter — because compression is invisible in the stew and criminal in the centerpiece. You compress by dish, not by policy. That is quantization. Mechanically, in one breath: weights trained at sixteen-bit float are stored in four to eight bits with per-block scale factors; memory drops proportionally, and inference actually speeds up on memory-bandwidth-bound hardware, because moving weights, not multiplying them, is the bottleneck. The uneven part — the part that makes this an engineering decision rather than a checkbox — is who pays quality. The LLM director pays least: schema-constrained planning at temperature zero is robust at Q4, because the constrained decoder masks most of the degradation surface, which is why quantize-the-text-brain-hardest is the default. Whisper at int8 is a solved trade, accuracy loss within noise for clean audio. TTS and voice conversion pay audibly earlier: quantization artifacts land in exactly the high-frequency texture the chain of ceilings runs through — a slightly grainy voice becomes slightly mushier lips downstream. And diffusion and rendering models pay most visibly: aggressive quantization shows up as texture smearing and detail loss in the one artifact the creator stares at. So the rule is not a rule but a procedure: golden sets per stage. A pinned set of reference inputs per model, reference outputs from the full-precision build, and per-stage comparison metrics — plan-property assertions for the director, the evaluation lesson's sync and quality scores for media. Sanji's proof is not opinion: before departure he cooks both versions of every compressed candidate side by side, the crew tastes blind, and anything the tasting flags travels whole. A quantization candidate ships when its golden run is within threshold — and the same harness gates model upgrades, making new-quant, new-model, new-version one decision pipeline instead of three vibes. Never ask does it seem fine; ask what is the measured delta on the pinned set. Nobody vibes.

The orchestration lesson's stage events are also the profiler: every render logs per-stage wall time, and the aggregate answers the only question that matters before optimizing — where do the minutes go? For talking-head pipelines the answer is nearly always the frames stage: per-frame neural rendering dominates, TTS and coefficients are seconds, mux is nothing. That immediately ranks the levers. Resolution is the big one: rendering at five-forty-p versus ten-eighty-p is roughly a four-times pixel difference in the dominant stage, and the preview-final split falls out naturally — edit-loop previews render at five-forty-p, the approved final at full resolution, so the creator iterates fast and ships pretty, the drag lesson's proxy-fidelity idea at pipeline scale. Chunked rendering, the frames stage processing in segments, bounds memory and enables the resume behavior the worker already supports. Precision, fp16 versus fp32 inference, is usually free speed on modern GPUs. And the director belongs on the CPU: llama.cpp runs an eight-B Q4 plan-emission in seconds on a desktop CPU, freeing the entire GPU for media — the rare case where slower hardware is the right architecture, because planning latency is off the render's critical path. The discipline attached: profile before and after every lever pull, on the same golden inputs, and keep the history — because optimizations interact. Chunking changed the frames stage's memory profile, which changed what could stay resident, which changed load-latency totals. And because the numbers rot as models upgrade. A pipeline that logs stage timings has this discipline nearly free; the only added ceremony is the before-and-after comparison being a habit instead of an accident.

Content-addressed caching made re-renders cheap; nothing made the cache small. Frames directories are hundreds of megabytes, WAVs add up, and a month of iterating creators fills any disk. Eviction is therefore designed, not discovered. At the crossing's staging island, the crew inherits the harbor warehouse's eviction rule from its ancient keeper: the warehouse fills every season, so what leaves is decided by a posted policy. Our policy has three parts. A size budget, a config value with a dashboard number behind it. LRU by last-use as the base policy, and the manifest already records access, because cache hits are uses. And pins for what must not vanish: artifacts of currently-open projects, and anything the consent or audit trail requires — enrollment artifacts are records, not cache, so they live outside the eviction domain entirely. Those are not storage, the keeper says of the pinned board; those are promises. But the refinement worth its complexity is this: evict by regeneration cost per megabyte, not just recency. A speaker embedding is kilobytes and cost a model load to make; coefficients are small and cost a minute; frames are huge and cost the most wall time but are also purely derivable from coefficients plus image, cheap-ish to remake at need; the final mux is derivable in seconds. So the policy keeps small expensive intermediates aggressively — embeddings, coefficients, TTS chunks — lets bulky rederivable artifacts go first, and never evicts below the active-project pin set. A barrel of fish I can throw out, the keeper says; the sea makes more fish. A surveyed chart of the reef passage took three men a month — that stays if I must sleep outside myself. You evict by what it costs to remake, not by what it costs to keep. This is also the answer to what we sync to the cloud in Part Six: the small expensive layer travels; the bulky derivable layer regenerates locally. One more echo of the course's oldest split — the sparse valuable representation versus the dense derivable one, the edit list versus the render, now as storage policy.

Step back and the whole fitting problem is one discipline applied three times: a measured budget, plus a written policy, plus an instrumented mechanism — at VRAM, at GPU-time, and at disk. Two levels down, the quantization details reward knowing. The llama.cpp K-quants use super-blocks with two-level scaling and mixed precision across tensor types — attention and output layers, measured as more sensitive, get more bits, the feed-forward bulk gets fewer, and the S, M, L suffixes trade that mix. Importance-aware variants run calibration data through the full model to spend the bit budget where it matters, meaningfully better below Q4 and marginal above it. And the non-weight gigabytes — the ledger's working-memory line — unpack into the KV cache, roughly half a megabyte per token of context for an eight-B model, so a four-thousand-token grounding block holds about two gigabytes on top of weights, and the activations, which for rendering models scale with resolution squared, which is exactly why the OOM fix clamps inputs at intake. The headroom line in every budget is these, given a number, and it is one and a half gigabytes rather than a hundred and fifty megabytes precisely because three of the four are input-shaped and only reveal their maximum on the worst upload a creator will eventually send. Finally, the product-review question: why does our app need twelve gigabytes when a competitor runs in the browser? The honest answer is that the competitor's models run on their servers — the VRAM did not disappear, it moved to a datacenter and onto a subscription bill, along with the user's face and voice biometrics; our local-first architecture is a privacy feature precisely because the twelve gigabytes is the user's own. And the number is negotiable, with prices: the director already runs on CPU, deeper quantization trades golden-set-gated quality, a batch-only tier sequences stages in about six gigabytes. The right question is not why twelve but which tier of hardware we serve at which quality — and the ledger, the golden sets, and the residency policies are exactly the machinery that lets us answer that as a product decision with measured tradeoffs, instead of a defensive number.
keyframes-timelines-motion-paths: Nami teaches navigation as three stacked disciplines, and the first is the oldest idea in animation. A route, she says, is drawn as a handful of marked waypoints — round this cape, sight this lighthouse, make this bearing by dusk — and the helmsman fills in the smooth sailing between them. That is exactly what a keyframe is: a value pinned at a time, like head pitch equal to minus three tenths at two seconds, with interpolation computing every frame in between. Senior animators drew the key poses; assistants drew the in-betweens — literally called inbetweening — and the idea survives because sparsity is precisely what editability means. A two-second nod is three keys a human can see, grab, and move: shift the middle key later and the nod lands later, raise its value and the nod deepens. Fifty per-frame numbers have nothing a hand can hold. As Nami puts it, the route you can grab is the route you can fix. And her curves between waypoints are eased, never angular — ships have mass, and a route with corners was drawn by someone who never stood at a helm. Linear interpolation gets you there at constant velocity with a visible corner at every key, fine for mechanical values and wrong for bodies; eased curves accelerate and decelerate the way mass does, which is why every animation tool defaults to them. The one discipline that keeps this honest: easing is a property saved on the segment, because the editor two lessons from now must draw a curve that is exactly what the renderer will compute. If the curve you drag differs from the curve that renders, drag-to-fix quietly becomes drag-to-lie.

Nami's second discipline is the overlay sheets, and it maps onto the timeline's two layers, which must never be confused. The dense base layer is the master chart: the talking-head model's output — per-frame coefficient streams, twenty-five values a second per channel, for speech articulation and aliveness. It is machine-made and machine-scaled; no human will ever hand-edit frame one thousand eight hundred forty-seven of mouth-open, and that is fine, because it regenerates from audio whenever the speech changes. The sparse editable layer is everything drawn on the transparent sheets pinned over that master: gesture instances — a wave at two seconds, amplitude one point two, from the description lesson — and manual fixes, keyframes a creator drags into place. Human-made, human-scaled, all of it grabbable. The organizing structures are channels, one value over time like head pitch or brow raise, grouped into tracks, and clips. And here is the load-bearing choice: a gesture is a clip that owns its template keyframes. Drag the clip and the whole gesture moves; stretch it and it retimes; its amplitude stays attached to the clip rather than smeared across raw keys. At render time the composite is dense base, plus instantiated gesture clips, plus manual edit keys, merged with the additive-clamped rules the gesture lab already built. The two-layer split is exactly why an edited project survives regeneration — the property Nami prizes most. When the Log Pose resets and the base chart must be redrawn for a new island, every overlay sheet pins straight onto the new master; the corrections outlive the chart. Change the script, the dense layer re-renders from new audio, and every gesture and fix re-applies on top. Edits outlive the base they decorate.

Nami's apprentice supplies the third discipline by getting it wrong. Some motion is spatial: a pointing hand travels an arc across the frame, a gaze target sweeps from viewer to product, a head traces a small path. You represent that trajectory as a motion path — a parametric curve, typically Bézier segments: anchor points the path passes through, with handles shaping the curve between them. The creator will see it next lesson as a literal draggable line over the video; the data is just anchors and handles, each anchor optionally pinned to a time. And here is the trap every implementer falls into exactly once. The apprentice plots a curve through a strait and marks arrival times evenly along his pen strokes — but the curve's middle section, bent wide by his flourish, holds twice the actual distance of its ends. Stepping a Bézier's parameter t evenly, zero, zero point one, zero point two, does not advance evenly along the curve; segments where the handles stretch the curve pack more distance into the same parameter step. So his evenly-marked times have the ship inexplicably sprinting through the middle of the strait and crawling at the ends, for no visible reason. Nami's fix is a knotted string: she measures the curve's actual length — sample and sum, numerically — then maps time to distance traveled, so motion covers equal distance per equal time unless an easing curve deliberately says otherwise. Every animation library does this internally; you need to know it exists because the bug — why does the point speed up mid-path when I bend the curve — will otherwise cost you an afternoon. And because arc length is the bridge that lets timing and geometry become independently editable. The pen's pace is not the ship's pace. Measure the string, then hang the hours on it.

This is the decision everything else leans on, and Nami's apprentice stages the disaster that proves it.
